event: Polynesian voyagers visit and temporarily inhabit several equatorial islands such as Kingman Reef area and the Line Islands, leaving archaeological traces of fishing camps and shrines.
Spanish navigator Alvaro de Mendana sights Wake Island during his Pacific expedition, marking one of the earliest recorded European encounters in the region.
American sea captain Simon Metcalfe names Navassa Island while sailing in the Caribbean, though the uninhabited islet had long been known to mariners.
British sea captain Samuel Wake of the trading ship Prince William Henry visits and lends his name to Wake Island.
American Captain Edmund Fanning sights Palmyra Atoll, beginning its association with United States interests.
The American ship Betsy is wrecked on Palmyra Atoll, an early documented shipwreck on the remote reef.
American whalers and sealers begin frequenting Johnston Atoll and other central Pacific islets in search of resources.
American Captain Joseph Allen sights and reports Baker Island in the equatorial Pacific.
Captain Obed Starbuck reports sighting Jarvis Island, later named for British shipowner Edward Jarvis.
Charles Wilkes of the United States Exploring Expedition surveys several central Pacific islands, expanding American geographic knowledge of the region.

The United States Congress passes the Guano Islands Act, allowing Americans to claim uninhabited guano-rich islands, which underpins later US claims to Baker, Howland, Jarvis, Johnston, Navassa, and others.
American companies claim Baker, Howland, and Jarvis Islands under the Guano Islands Act and begin mining phosphate-rich guano deposits.
The United States formally claims Johnston Atoll and Navassa Island under the Guano Islands Act, initiating guano extraction operations.
Kingman Reef is recorded and named for Captain W. E. Kingman, who described the largely submerged atoll.
Large-scale guano mining is underway on Navassa Island, worked largely by African American laborers under harsh conditions.
A labor uprising erupts on Navassa Island as Black workers rebel against brutal conditions, killing several supervisors and triggering a landmark US Supreme Court case (Jones v. United States).
The United States annexes Wake Island during the Spanish-American War era, recognizing its strategic value as a Pacific waystation.
The US Navy formally takes possession of Wake Island, and the United States asserts sovereignty over Kingman Reef.
A US Marine guard is briefly stationed and a cable relay considered for Wake Island as transpacific communication routes develop.
Palmyra Atoll is formally annexed by the United States and administered as part of the Territory of Hawaii.
Johnston Atoll and Wake Island are placed under US Navy control by executive order, reinforcing their military significance.
The United States begins colonizing Baker, Howland, and Jarvis Islands with American settlers under the American Equatorial Islands Colonization Project to strengthen sovereignty claims.
President Franklin Roosevelt formally places Baker, Howland, and Jarvis Islands under US jurisdiction administered by the Interior Department.
An airstrip is built on Howland Island to support aviator Amelia Earhart, who disappears nearby during her attempted round-the-world flight.
Pan American Airways establishes a flying-boat refueling station and hotel on Wake Island for its transpacific Clipper service.
Midway Atoll is developed as a major US naval air station as tensions rise across the Pacific.
Japanese forces attack and capture Wake Island following a fierce defense by US Marines, and bombard Midway and other islands at the outbreak of the Pacific War.
The Battle of Midway becomes a decisive US naval victory over Japan, turning the tide of World War II in the Pacific; American colonists are evacuated from the equatorial islands.
Japanese troops on Wake Island massacre 98 captured American civilian laborers, an atrocity later prosecuted as a war crime.
Japanese forces on Wake Island surrender to the United States at the end of World War II.
Johnston Atoll becomes a site for US high-altitude nuclear weapons testing during the Cold War.
A series of high-altitude nuclear tests, including the Starfish Prime detonation, is conducted from Johnston Atoll, with several failed launches scattering plutonium contamination.
Johnston Atoll is designated for storage of chemical weapons, becoming a major US chemical munitions stockpile site.
Wake Island serves as a refuge processing point, foreshadowing its later humanitarian role.
Wake Island is used to temporarily house tens of thousands of Vietnamese refugees during Operation New Life following the fall of Saigon.
Sovereignty over much of the central Pacific is reorganized as nearby trust territories move toward independence, leaving the scattered US islands as unincorporated possessions.
The Johnston Atoll Chemical Agent Disposal System begins incinerating stockpiled US chemical weapons.
Construction of a chemical weapons incinerator and cleanup operations near completion on Johnston Atoll as destruction of the stockpile proceeds.
The Palmyra Atoll is purchased largely by The Nature Conservancy to protect its pristine coral reef and tropical ecosystem.
Destruction of the chemical weapons stockpile on Johnston Atoll is completed, and the military facilities are slated for closure and environmental remediation.
President George W. Bush designates the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument, protecting Baker, Howland, Jarvis, Johnston, Kingman Reef, Palmyra, and Wake waters.
President Barack Obama expands the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument to roughly 490,000 square miles, creating one of the world's largest marine protected areas.
Ongoing conservation efforts focus on eradicating invasive species and restoring seabird and coral habitats across the wildlife refuges.
Scientific studies document the vulnerability of the low-lying atolls to sea-level rise and coral bleaching driven by climate change.
Federal agencies continue managing the islands as national wildlife refuges and marine monuments while debate persists over the disputed status of Navassa Island, also claimed by Haiti.
